title: toNodeReadable: 'close' event and generator finally both race to cancel session on non-clean exit
description: The `buffers()` generator sets `finished = true` only on the happy path (line 89). On any other exit the `finally` block (line 92-94) checks `!finished`, sets it, and fires `session.cancel`. The `stream.on('close', ...)` handler (line 97-101) does the same check. Because `Readable.from` calls `iterator.return()` asynchronously after emitting 'close', both paths can observe `finished === false` before either has a chance to set it. In practice on Node's event loop the 'close' event fires synchronously on `readable.destroy()`, while `iterator.return()` is scheduled as a microtask, so the close handler always wins and the generator finally also fires — the idempotent guard on `finished` (set before the cancel call) prevents double-cancel only if both paths run synchronously in the same tick. Since the cancel in the close handler (`void session.cancel(...)`) is fire-and-forget, the flag is set synchronously, protecting the generator finally. However the reverse is not true: if `Readable.from` calls `iterator.return()` first (e.g. during normal consumer exhaustion that then triggers close), the close handler checks `finished` before the generator finally has set it, causing a duplicate cancel.

title: fromNodeReadable: onAbort fires a floating promise that outlives the function's finally block
description: The `onAbort` handler on line 26 does `void session.cancel(ABORT_REASON).finally(() => { readable.destroy(); })`. This chain is never awaited. The `signal.removeEventListener('abort', onAbort)` in `finally` (line 72) prevents future firings but cannot recall an already-dispatched chain. If the abort signal fires mid-loop (e.g. during `await session.push(chunk)` on line 58), the cancel-then-destroy chain runs concurrently alongside the loop body. The loop body will then also call `session.cancel` in its own catch path (line 69). The idempotent guard is NOT present in `fromNodeReadable` — there is no `cancelled` flag — so `session.cancel` can be called twice with different reasons. Additionally, `fromNodeReadable` can return/throw before `session.cancel().finally(destroy)` completes, meaning the caller sees resolution before teardown is done.

category: use-after-error / unhandled rejection on aborted stream
title: toReadableStream pull: abort fires controller.error() while pull is awaiting iterator.next(), enqueue/close on already-errored controller throws unhandled TypeError
description: In `toReadableStream`, `abortHandler` (line 184-187) calls `controller.error(...)` synchronously from the abort event. If `pull()` is currently suspended at `await iterator.next()` (line 203) when the abort fires, the controller transitions to 'errored'. When `iterator.next()` resolves, the pull resumes and attempts `controller.close()` (line 212) or `controller.enqueue(value)` (line 214) — both throw a `TypeError: Cannot call close/enqueue on a ReadableStream that is already closed or errored`. This TypeError is thrown inside the `try` block but AFTER the `await`, in a code path that does not re-check `signal?.aborted` before calling those methods. The catch block (line 216) then calls `controller.error(e)` on an already-errored controller, which throws a second uncaught TypeError. The net result is an uncaught exception inside the underlying source's pull callback.

title: fromReadableStream inner loop checks signal.aborted before await but not before session.push, leaving a window where abort is missed for one chunk
description: The abort check `if (signal?.aborted) throw new DOMException(...)` on line 98 occurs at the top of the while loop before `await pending`. After the abort check passes and `await pending` resolves with a chunk, the signal may have fired during the await. The code does not re-check `signal?.aborted` before calling `await session.push(chunk)` (line 125). This means one chunk can be pushed to the session after abort. The existing post-loop abort check at line 133 handles the normal-completion path but not the mid-push interleave. This is a minor behavioral inconsistency — the session receives one extra chunk when abort races the push — rather than a safety-critical issue.

title: toNodeReadable: no test for abort-signal mid-stream cancellation path
description: The test suite covers consumer destroy (line 67-87) and clean completion but has no test that fires an AbortSignal while toNodeReadable is emitting chunks. The Readable.from `signal` option is exercised indirectly but the session.cancel path triggered via signal abort is untested. Without this test the double-cancel race (concurrency-1) and abort ordering in the buffers() generator cannot be regression-tested.

title: toReadableStream: no test coverage at all; abort-during-pull race (concurrency-3) is untested
description: The test files cover fromReadableStream, fromByteRange, fromRangePrefix, and fromNodeReadable, but there is zero test coverage for toReadableStream (browser.ts:157-236). The identified abort-during-pull race (concurrency-3) cannot be caught by any current test. A minimal test should: start iteration, enqueue one chunk, trigger abort via controller, and verify no uncaught TypeError and that session.cancel is called exactly once.
